questions.question: Why is requirement ranking necessary in a senior design interview?
options: Because most real systems cannot optimize every important goal equally, so architecture must reflect priorities.
Because interviewers mainly care about whether requirements are written in the right order.
Because ranking requirements removes the need for trade-off discussion.
Because only low-latency systems need prioritization.
questions.explanation: Requirement ranking is how the candidate makes design trade-offs visible and defensible.

questions.question: What is a sign of weak prioritization?
options: Stating that every requirement is critical and leaving the architecture to resolve the conflict implicitly.
Choosing two top priorities and deferring some secondary goals.
Explaining which requirement wins if availability and consistency conflict.
Changing the ranking when a business constraint changes.
questions.explanation: If everything is critical, nothing is truly prioritized.

questions.question: Which design statement shows real priority framing?
options: Because freshness matters more than read latency here, I will keep cache TTLs short and accept more origin load.
I will optimize for scale, reliability, consistency, and low cost at the same time.
I prefer queues in most systems, so I will add one here too.
We can settle priorities after the architecture is complete.
questions.explanation: A good statement links a ranked requirement directly to an architectural consequence.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off risk when low latency becomes the top goal?
options: Freshness, write complexity, or cost may worsen depending on how latency is achieved.
The system can no longer use APIs at all.
Observability stops mattering because user experience becomes fast.
Multi-region failover becomes free.
questions.explanation: Latency optimization usually introduces real costs elsewhere.

questions.question: Why should deferred requirements be named explicitly?
options: Because they show scope control and prevent the design from pretending to solve everything now.
Because deferred requirements can never become important later.
Because interviewers subtract points for every omitted optional feature.
Because deferral eliminates the need for future rollout planning.
questions.explanation: Explicit deferral is often more senior than hidden omission.

questions.question: Which observability signal would best reveal whether the chosen priority is being met?
options: A metric tied directly to the winning requirement, such as freshness lag for a freshness-first design.
Only the total line count of the implementation.
The number of architecture boxes in the diagram.
A generic dashboard with no breakdown by workload or failure mode.
questions.explanation: Observability should align with the system's most important objective.

questions.question: If compliance suddenly becomes a top priority, what should happen first?
options: The requirement ranking should change and any affected data retention, audit, or deletion decisions should be revisited.
The original architecture should remain untouched to preserve consistency of explanation.
Only the UI layer needs to change.
Trade-offs no longer matter once compliance is involved.
questions.explanation: A changed priority should lead to concrete design updates, not a cosmetic acknowledgment.

questions.question: What most strongly distinguishes prioritization from mere requirement listing?
options: Prioritization explains which goal wins in a conflict and what design consequence follows from that choice.
Prioritization simply means listing functional requirements before non-functional ones.
Prioritization avoids discussing any costs so the design stays optimistic.
Prioritization is only relevant for storage and caching topics.
questions.explanation: The value of prioritization is in conflict resolution and the decisions it forces.
